q: What's the difference between precision and recall when evaluating a retriever, and why can't you optimize just one?
a: Precision is the fraction of retrieved chunks that are actually relevant; recall is the fraction of all relevant chunks that you managed to retrieve. They trade off: returning more chunks raises recall but usually lowers precision, and a tiny top-k boosts precision but can miss context the generator needs. You pick the operating point based on whether the downstream LLM is hurt more by missing evidence (favor recall) or by distractor noise (favor precision).
fs: Frames it as a tunable trade-off tied to downstream generator behavior, not a single number to maximize.
fj: Says 'just maximize accuracy' or conflates precision and recall as the same retrieval-quality metric.

q: Define faithfulness in RAG evaluation and explain how a faithfulness score is actually computed.
a: Faithfulness measures whether the generated answer is grounded in the retrieved context, i.e. every claim it makes is supported by the passages, independent of whether the answer is also correct. The standard mechanism decomposes the answer into atomic claims, then uses an NLI model or LLM judge to label each claim as entailed, neutral, or contradicted by the context; the score is the fraction of claims that are entailed. A fluent, factually-true answer can still score low on faithfulness if its facts came from the model's parametric memory rather than the retrieved context.
fs: Separates faithfulness (grounded-in-context) from correctness, and names the claim-decomposition + NLI-entailment mechanism.
fj: Treats faithfulness as 'is the answer correct' and proposes a single embedding-similarity score.

q: In RAGAS, what do context precision and context recall measure, and what does each require as input?
a: Context precision measures whether the relevant retrieved chunks are ranked near the top (a rank-aware signal that the right context appears early), while context recall measures whether the retrieved context covers everything needed to produce the ground-truth answer. Context recall specifically needs a reference answer (or reference contexts) as ground truth, because it checks how many ground-truth claims are attributable to the retrieved context. Context precision can be computed reference-free by judging chunk relevance to the question, which makes recall the harder metric to run in production without labels.
fs: Knows context recall is the labeled, ground-truth-dependent metric and that precision is rank-aware.
fj: Describes both as generic 'how good is retrieval' with no mention of needing ground truth.

q: What is an LLM-as-a-judge, and why is it so widely used despite being imperfect?
a: An LLM-as-a-judge uses a strong model, prompted with a rubric, to score or compare other models' outputs in place of (or to scale up) human raters. It's popular because it's far cheaper and faster than human annotation, handles open-ended outputs that exact-match can't grade, and correlates reasonably with human preference on many tasks. The catch is that it inherits its own biases and must be validated against human labels before you trust it as ground truth.
fs: Justifies it as a scalable proxy for human judgment that must itself be calibrated to humans.
fj: Treats the judge's score as objective truth and never mentions validating it against humans.

q: Name the main biases an LLM judge exhibits and one mitigation for each.
a: Position bias: the judge favors the first (or last) answer in a pairwise prompt, mitigated by running both orderings and averaging. Verbosity/length bias: it prefers longer, more fluent answers regardless of substance, mitigated by length normalization or explicit rubric penalties. Self-enhancement (self-preference) bias: it rates outputs from its own model family higher, mitigated by using a different judge model than the one under test and masking model identity. You can also reduce variance generally by giving few-shot calibrated examples in the rubric.
fs: Names position, verbosity, and self-preference bias with a concrete mitigation each.
fj: Says 'LLM judges are mostly fine' or only vaguely warns they 'can be biased.'

code.label: kill position bias
lines: # only count a win if it

q: Your team uses GPT-4 to judge outputs from your GPT-4-based product. What specific risk does that create and how do you handle it?
a: Self-preference bias: a judge tends to rate text from its own model family higher, partly because it favors low-perplexity outputs that look familiar to it, so the judge is systematically biased toward your own system and inflates scores. The fix is to use a judge from a different model family (or an ensemble of judges), blind the judge to which system produced each output, and validate the judge's verdicts against a human-labeled gold set to measure the residual bias. If you must self-judge, at least report the gap between the judge's scores and human scores so the bias is visible.
fs: Names self-preference bias and proposes a cross-family judge plus human calibration.
fj: Sees no problem with a model grading its own family's outputs.

q: What is a golden (eval) set and what properties make a good one?
a: A golden set is a curated, human-trusted collection of inputs with reference answers or labels that you treat as ground truth for gating and regression detection. A good one is representative of real traffic, covers hard and edge cases (not just the easy majority), is labeled with high inter-annotator agreement, and is version-controlled so results are comparable over time. It should be large enough for statistical power but small enough to label carefully, and held out from anything used to tune prompts or models.
fs: Calls out representativeness, edge-case coverage, labeling agreement, and versioning/holdout.
fj: Thinks a golden set is just 'some example inputs we tried once.'

q: How do you measure and improve inter-annotator agreement when building a labeled eval set, and why does it matter?
a: Use Cohen's kappa for two raters or Krippendorff's alpha when you have multiple raters with missing labels, and weighted kappa for ordinal/Likert scales so near-misses are penalized less than far ones; report the coefficient, not just raw percent agreement, because chance agreement inflates raw numbers. Low agreement means the rubric is ambiguous, so you tighten definitions, add positive and negative examples with explanations, and adjudicate disagreements before finalizing labels. It matters because your golden labels are the ceiling on any judge's measurable quality: if humans don't agree, no automated metric can be validated against them.
fs: Picks the right coefficient per scale type and treats low agreement as a rubric-design bug.
fj: Reports raw percent agreement and never connects it to rubric ambiguity.

q: What is regression testing for an LLM application and how is it different from regression testing normal software?
a: It's re-running your eval set on every prompt, model, or retrieval change to catch quality drops before they ship, typically gating a merge in CI. It differs from normal software regression tests because outputs are non-deterministic and not exact-match, so you assert on metric thresholds (faithfulness, judge score, pass rate) and aggregate over many cases rather than checking one deterministic output. You also have to account for noise: a single flaky case shouldn't fail the build, so you compare distributions or set thresholds with margin.
fs: Frames it as threshold/distribution assertions in CI, accounting for non-determinism.
fj: Expects exact-string assertions like a unit test and fails on any output change.

q: A prompt tweak improved your average judge score from 4.1 to 4.2 on 50 examples. Would you ship it?
a: Not on that evidence alone, because 0.1 on a 1-5 scale over only 50 noisy, judge-scored examples is almost certainly within noise. I'd compute a confidence interval or run a paired test (the same examples scored under both prompts, e.g. paired t-test or Wilcoxon signed-rank) and bootstrap the difference to see if it's significant. I'd also slice by subpopulation to check the average isn't hiding a regression on an important segment, and confirm the gain is practically meaningful, not just statistically detectable.
fs: Demands a paired significance test and CI before trusting a small average delta.
fj: Ships immediately because 'the number went up.'

q: How does SelfCheckGPT detect hallucinations without any reference or external knowledge?
a: It samples multiple stochastic completions for the same prompt and measures how consistent they are with the main answer, on the premise that genuinely-known facts stay stable across samples while hallucinated ones vary. Sentences that contradict or aren't supported by the other samples get a high hallucination score; the NLI-based variant (checking entailment between the answer and each sample) is the most effective and efficient of the implementations. It's reference-free, which makes it usable for online monitoring where you have no ground truth.
fs: Explains the consistency-across-samples premise and the NLI variant, and why it's reference-free.
fj: Thinks you always need a ground-truth source to detect hallucination.

q: Distinguish a faithfulness (grounding) failure from a factual hallucination, and why the distinction changes your detection strategy.
a: A grounding failure means the answer contradicts or isn't supported by the retrieved context, even if the claim happens to be true in the world; a factual hallucination means the claim is false against world knowledge regardless of context. For grounding you can detect cheaply with NLI between answer claims and the retrieved passages (no external knowledge needed), which is ideal for RAG. For world-factuality you need an external knowledge source or retrieval-based fact-checking (e.g. FActScore-style verification against a corpus), which is more expensive and is what you reach for when there's no trusted context to ground against.
fs: Separates 'unsupported by context' from 'false in the world' and maps each to the right detector.
fj: Uses 'hallucination' for everything and proposes one generic checker.

q: Walk through the components you'd evaluate in a RAG pipeline and which metric isolates each.
a: Split it into retrieval and generation: context precision/recall isolate the retriever (did the right chunks come back and rank high), while faithfulness and answer relevancy isolate the generator (did it stay grounded and actually address the question). The trick is that a bad end-to-end answer could be either component's fault, so you measure them separately to localize the bug. For example, high context recall but low faithfulness points at the generator ignoring good context, whereas low context recall points at retrieval.
fs: Decomposes the pipeline and uses the metric pattern to localize which component failed.
fj: Only reports one end-to-end answer-quality number with no component attribution.

q: Answer relevancy in RAGAS can be high even when the answer is wrong. Why, and what does that imply?
a: Answer relevancy measures how well the answer addresses the question (often by generating questions from the answer and comparing them to the original), not whether the answer is correct or grounded. So a confident, on-topic but factually wrong answer scores well on relevancy. The implication is you must never use relevancy alone as a quality gate; pair it with faithfulness for grounding and a correctness check against ground truth to catch the wrong-but-relevant case.
fs: Knows relevancy measures on-topic-ness, not correctness, and insists on pairing metrics.
fj: Assumes high relevancy means the answer is good.

q: How is evaluating an agent fundamentally different from evaluating a single LLM call?
a: An agent produces a multi-step trajectory of tool calls and reasoning, so you evaluate not just the final answer but the path: did it call the right tools with the right arguments, in a sensible order, without unnecessary or looping steps. That means three levels of metrics: end-to-end task success, trajectory quality (tool-call accuracy, step efficiency, progress rate vs the expected path), and component-level checks to pinpoint which tool or sub-agent broke. A correct final answer reached by a broken or wasteful trajectory is still a problem in production because of cost, latency, and fragility.
fs: Evaluates the trajectory and tool-calls at multiple levels, not just the final answer.
fj: Only checks whether the final answer is right, ignoring the path taken.

q: How would you evaluate an agent's trajectory, and what's the difference between exact-match and LLM-judged trajectory evaluation?
a: You compare the agent's actual sequence of tool calls and arguments against a reference trajectory. Exact-match (or in-order/any-order set match) checks whether the called tools match the expected ones deterministically, which is strict and cheap but brittle when multiple valid paths exist. An LLM-judge trajectory eval instead reasons about whether the path was reasonable and goal-directed even if it differs from the reference, which tolerates valid alternative solutions but costs more and adds judge noise. Use exact-match when there's one correct tool sequence, and an LLM judge when many valid paths exist.
fs: Contrasts deterministic trajectory match vs LLM-judge and picks based on path uniqueness.
fj: Assumes there's exactly one correct trajectory and only string-matches tool names.

q: Explain offline vs online evaluation for an LLM product and when each is used.
a: Offline evaluation runs a fixed, curated dataset with reference answers in a controlled setting, typically before deploy and in CI, so you can use reference-based metrics and catch regressions pre-merge. Online evaluation scores live production traffic in real time, where you have no ground truth, so you rely on reference-free signals (toxicity classifiers, faithfulness checks, user feedback, guardrails). They're complementary: offline catches known regressions before shipping, online surfaces drift, abuse, and distribution shift you didn't anticipate.
fs: Maps offline to reference-based pre-deploy gating and online to reference-free live monitoring.
fj: Treats them as interchangeable or thinks offline eval alone is sufficient.

q: Why can a model that wins offline still lose in an online A/B test?
a: Offline metrics measure proxy quality on a static, possibly stale dataset, while the A/B test measures real user outcomes under live distribution, latency, and interaction effects your eval set never captured. Distribution shift (real prompts differ from your golden set), proxy-metric misalignment (judge score up but users don't convert), and operational costs like added latency can all flip the result. This is why you validate offline-online correlation and treat offline eval as a fast filter, not the final word; the A/B test on the real KPI is the arbiter.
fs: Cites distribution shift and proxy-vs-real-KPI gap, and trusts the online KPI as arbiter.
fj: Assumes a better offline score guarantees a better product.

q: When A/B testing an LLM change, what are primary vs guardrail metrics and why do you need both?
a: The primary metric is the outcome you're trying to move (e.g. task completion or user retention), while guardrail metrics are things you must not regress (latency, cost per query, refusal rate, toxicity). You need both because an LLM change can lift the primary metric while quietly harming a guardrail; for example a more verbose, helpful answer might raise satisfaction but blow up latency and token cost. You only roll out if the treatment wins on the primary metric without violating any guardrail threshold.
fs: Pairs a primary KPI with explicit guardrails and gates rollout on not regressing them.
fj: Tracks only the headline metric and ignores cost/latency/safety side effects.

q: How do you decide sample size for an LLM A/B test, and why is it harder than a typical web experiment?
a: You run a power analysis from the baseline metric, the minimum effect you care about (MDE), and the metric's variance to find the sessions needed for, say, 80% power at alpha 0.05. It's harder for LLMs because outputs are stochastic and metrics like judge scores are noisier and often heavy-tailed, inflating variance and the required sample; under-powered tests fail to detect real wins. Reducing per-unit variance (deterministic decoding where possible, paired/within-subject designs, or variance-reduction like CUPED) lets you reach significance with fewer samples.
fs: Drives sample size from MDE and variance and notes LLM stochasticity inflates the requirement.
fj: Picks a round number of examples with no power analysis.

q: How do you design a good rubric for an LLM-as-a-judge, and should you use pointwise or pairwise scoring?
a: Make the rubric specific with discrete criteria, a constrained output format, and worked positive and negative examples with explanations, since concrete examples sharply raise agreement; vague 1-10 'quality' scales produce noisy, uncalibrated judgments. Pairwise (A vs B) is generally more reliable than pointwise because relative judgments are easier and more stable for both humans and judges, but it scales as O(n^2) comparisons and needs aggregation like Bradley-Terry/Elo to produce a ranking. Use pointwise with a clear rubric when you need an absolute threshold to gate on, and pairwise when you're comparing candidate systems.
fs: Builds a concrete example-driven rubric and chooses pairwise vs pointwise by the use case.
fj: Asks the judge to 'rate quality 1-10' with no criteria or examples.

q: Before you trust an LLM judge's scores, how do you validate the judge itself?
a: Treat the judge like any classifier: run it on a human-labeled gold set and measure agreement with the human labels (Cohen's/weighted kappa or correlation for ordinal scores), not just raw accuracy. Check it for the known biases by perturbing inputs (swap pairwise order, pad length, mask model identity) and confirm the verdict is stable. Only once agreement and bias are acceptable do you use it at scale, and you periodically re-validate because prompt or model upgrades can silently shift its behavior.
fs: Validates the judge against human gold with kappa/correlation and active bias probes.
fj: Assumes a frontier model is automatically a trustworthy judge with no validation.

q: What's the difference between reference-based and reference-free evaluation, and when do you reach for each?
a: Reference-based evaluation compares an output to a known correct answer, so it needs labeled ground truth and powers metrics like exact-match, BLEU/ROUGE, or correctness checks. Reference-free evaluation scores an output on its own (or against the input/context) with no gold label, using signals like faithfulness, toxicity, or an LLM-judge rubric. You use reference-based offline where you can afford labels and want a clean pass/fail gate, and reference-free online where production traffic has no ground truth. Most real systems run both: reference-based pre-deploy, reference-free in monitoring.
fs: Ties reference-based to labeled offline gating and reference-free to unlabeled online monitoring.
fj: Thinks every eval needs a ground-truth answer, so online monitoring is impossible.

q: Why have BLEU and ROUGE fallen out of favor for evaluating LLM outputs, and what replaced them?
a: BLEU and ROUGE score n-gram overlap with a reference, so they punish valid paraphrases and synonyms and reward surface copying, which correlates poorly with human judgment on open-ended generation. Embedding-based metrics like BERTScore improve on this by matching contextual token embeddings instead of exact n-grams, lifting human alignment, but they still miss document-level coherence and can be fooled by fluent hallucinations. For most modern LLM tasks teams have moved to LLM-as-judge rubrics and task-specific checks (faithfulness, correctness against claims), keeping n-gram metrics only for narrow tasks like translation where references are tight.
fs: Explains n-gram brittleness to paraphrase and names the embedding/LLM-judge successors with their own limits.
fj: Still treats a high BLEU/ROUGE score as proof the answer is good.

q: What is eval-driven development and how does it change the way you build an LLM feature?
a: Eval-driven development applies TDD thinking to LLM apps: you write the eval set and metrics first, then iterate prompts, retrieval, or models until they pass, rather than eyeballing a few outputs. The golden set is created and version-controlled at the start from real traffic, edge cases, and known failure modes, and every change is judged against it. This flips development from 'looks good to me' to a measurable loop, so you can refactor prompts confidently and prove a change helped. The discipline also forces you to define what 'good' means before you start tuning.
fs: Frames evals-first as a measurable loop with a versioned golden set defined before tuning.
fj: Builds the prompt first and only adds evals later (or never), judging by vibes.

q: A 'better' prompt passed your offline eval but users complained after rollout. What likely went wrong?
a: Most likely your golden set didn't cover the production cohort that broke, so the offline gain was real on a stale, narrow slice but missed real-world distribution. This is eval-score drift: prompt rollouts can pass offline yet degrade on traffic the eval never sampled. The fix is to mine the complaints back into the golden set, stratify it by real user intents, and add online reference-free guardrails plus an A/B test so the real KPI is the arbiter, not the offline proxy. You also re-check whether the 'better' prompt overfit to your eval examples.
fs: Diagnoses distribution gap/eval-score drift and feeds failures back into a stratified set plus online checks.
fj: Trusts the offline score as final and blames users instead of the eval coverage.

q: How do you size and stratify a golden eval set, and why isn't bigger always better?
a: A practical golden set is small and version-controlled, often 50-200 cases, stratified across the common user intents plus known edge cases and failure modes so the average isn't dominated by easy traffic. It must be large enough for statistical power on the effects you care about but small enough that humans can label it carefully with high agreement, since sloppy labels at scale just add noise. Bigger sets cost more to label and maintain and tempt you to over-trust an aggregate that hides per-slice regressions. You pair the lean deterministic golden gate with random production sampling to surface new failures.
fs: Calls for a stratified 50-200 case set balancing power against label quality, plus production sampling.
fj: Assumes a huge unlabeled or unstratified dump of examples is automatically a better eval set.

q: Your judge gives a clean average score but your best-of-N selection keeps picking worse answers. What's happening?
a: Good aggregate judge metrics don't guarantee good per-item decisions: best-of-N depends on the judge's ranking being correct on each comparison, and a judge can have decent mean calibration while its fine-grained ordering is noisy or tied. Recent work shows judge scores that look fine in aggregate still fail budgeted best-of-N audits because small score gaps fall inside the judge's noise. The fixes are pairwise comparison instead of pointwise scoring for selection, requiring a minimum margin or explicit uncertainty/confidence-interval width before trusting a pick, and resampling to break ties. Validate the judge specifically on the selection task, not just on average agreement.
fs: Separates aggregate calibration from per-item ranking reliability and fixes selection with pairwise/margin/uncertainty.
fj: Assumes a good average judge score means good best-of-N decisions.

q: How would you use an LLM judge to grade a classification task, and what metrics do you report?
a: When an LLM performs a labeling task (intent, sentiment, routing), you evaluate it like any classifier against a labeled set: report precision, recall, and F1 per class, plus a confusion matrix, not just overall accuracy, because class imbalance makes accuracy misleading. Pick the operating point by which error is costlier (recall when misses are dangerous, precision when false positives are expensive), and use macro-F1 to weight rare classes equally. If the LLM emits free-text labels you first normalize them to the label set before scoring. Track per-class metrics over time so a model upgrade can't silently wreck one rare but important class.
fs: Treats the LLM as a classifier with per-class precision/recall/F1 and confusion matrix, not bare accuracy.
fj: Reports a single accuracy number and ignores class imbalance and per-class behavior.

q: How do you evaluate structured/JSON output from an LLM, and why isn't 'it parsed' enough?
a: Structured-output eval has layers: schema-validity rate (does it parse and conform to the JSON schema), field-level correctness against ground truth, and downstream task success once the data is consumed. 'It parsed' only proves syntactic validity, not that the values are right or that required fields are populated, so a syntactically valid but semantically wrong object can still trigger a bad refund or action. You measure each field's accuracy, track the validity rate as a guardrail, and for action-triggering outputs treat schema compliance as a hard requirement enforced by constrained decoding. Report where failures land: malformed, valid-but-wrong-field, or valid-but-incomplete.
fs: Separates schema-validity from field-level correctness and downstream success, treating compliance as a hard gate.
fj: Considers the eval passed as soon as the JSON parses, ignoring field correctness.

q: Walk me through running a red-team / safety evaluation on an LLM product. What do you measure?
a: You assemble adversarial probes across harm categories (jailbreaks, prompt injection, PII leakage, toxic or biased output, unsafe tool actions) and run them through the system, measuring attack success rate per category and overall. Modern practice uses automated red-team agents that generate and mutate attacks (roleplay, crescendo, encoding tricks), which reach high success rates and scale far past hand-written prompts. You report ASR by attack type, severity beyond a binary pass/fail, and time-to-jailbreak, then track these as guardrail metrics that must not regress across model and prompt upgrades. Red-teaming is continuous and gated in the deploy pipeline, not a one-time audit.
fs: Frames safety eval as per-category attack-success-rate from automated adversarial agents, run continuously.
fj: Thinks a handful of manual 'try to break it' prompts once is sufficient safety coverage.

q: What is noise sensitivity in RAG evaluation and why does it matter beyond faithfulness?
a: Noise sensitivity measures how much answer quality degrades when irrelevant or distractor chunks are retrieved alongside the relevant ones, capturing whether the generator gets misled by junk in its context. It matters because a system can score well on faithfulness and recall in clean conditions yet fall apart in production where retrieval inevitably returns some noise. High noise sensitivity points you at the generator (or at tightening retrieval/reranking to cut distractors), distinct from a pure recall miss. Tracking it stops you from shipping a pipeline that only works when retrieval is perfect.
fs: Defines noise sensitivity as quality degradation under distractor chunks and ties it to generator robustness.
fj: Assumes good faithfulness on clean context means the system is robust to retrieval noise.

q: Context entities recall and answer correctness in RAGAS — what do they add over the core four metrics?
a: Context entities recall checks the fraction of ground-truth entities (names, dates, IDs) present in the retrieved context, a sharper retrieval signal for fact-heavy queries where missing one entity sinks the answer even if overall recall looks fine. Answer correctness combines factual similarity to the reference with semantic similarity, giving a single reference-based 'is the final answer right' score that faithfulness and relevancy alone don't provide, since those measure grounding and on-topic-ness, not truth. Together they cover the gaps: entities recall localizes a specific retrieval miss, correctness gates end-to-end truth against ground truth. Both need labeled references, so they're offline metrics.
fs: Maps entities recall to fact-level retrieval misses and answer correctness to reference-based end-to-end truth.
fj: Treats faithfulness and relevancy as already covering whether the final answer is correct.

q: An agent calls every required tool correctly but still produces an answer a domain expert would reject. How does your eval catch this?
a: Tool-call accuracy is necessary but not sufficient: an agent can hit 100% on tool selection and arguments yet violate policy or synthesize the results poorly. So you evaluate at three levels — task success (did the end goal get met to expert standard), trajectory quality (tool accuracy, step efficiency, progress rate toward the goal), and a final-output quality judge against a rubric or expert-labeled gold. The expert-rejection case is caught by the outcome and output-quality layers, not the trajectory layer, which is exactly why you can't stop at tool metrics. You also slice failures to localize whether planning, a specific tool, or synthesis broke.
fs: Adds outcome and output-quality layers above tool-call accuracy to catch correct-tools-but-bad-answer.
fj: Declares the agent good because tool-call accuracy is 100%.

q: What is progress rate as an agent metric, and why prefer it over a binary task-success flag?
a: Progress rate compares the agent's actual trajectory against the expected one to measure how far toward the goal it got, rather than a single pass/fail at the end. It's more informative because most agent runs partially succeed, and a binary flag throws away the signal that distinguishes 'reached step 4 of 5 then looped' from 'failed immediately', which matters for debugging and for detecting small regressions. On long multi-step tasks where end-to-end success is rare and noisy, progress rate gives a denser, lower-variance metric to optimize and to gate on. You still keep task success as the headline, but progress rate tells you where it's breaking.
fs: Defines progress rate as fractional advance vs expected path and values its denser signal over binary success.
fj: Only logs whether the whole task passed, discarding partial-progress information.

q: Frontier providers silently change model behavior even on pinned IDs. How do you keep your evals trustworthy across that drift?
a: Treat the model as a moving target: even version-stamped IDs have shifted behavior between dates, so you schedule your reference golden set to re-run on a cadence (and on every provider update) and alert on any score degradation as a drift signal. You separate prompt drift, model drift, and eval-score drift so you know whether the input, the model, or your metric moved. Pin model versions where the provider allows it, log model IDs with every eval run for attribution, and re-validate your LLM judge after upgrades since the judge itself drifts. The curated reference dataset run on a schedule is the gold-standard drift detector.
fs: Schedules golden-set reruns as a drift detector, distinguishes prompt/model/eval drift, and re-validates the judge.
fj: Assumes a pinned model ID is frozen, so evals never need re-running until you change code.

q: How do you treat cost and latency as first-class evaluation dimensions rather than afterthoughts?
a: Quality isn't free, so you score every candidate on cost-per-query (tokens in/out times price) and latency (p50/p95), and gate rollout on these as guardrails alongside the quality metric. A change that lifts judge score but doubles p95 latency or token cost may be a net loss in production, especially for agents where extra steps multiply both. You plot a quality-vs-cost frontier to pick the operating point instead of chasing maximum quality blindly, and you set hard latency/cost ceilings so a verbose 'better' answer can't silently blow the budget. Reporting them per change keeps the trade-off explicit in ship decisions.
fs: Makes cost-per-query and p95 latency guardrails and reasons on a quality-vs-cost frontier.
fj: Optimizes only for quality scores and ignores token cost and latency until production bills arrive.

q: How do you keep LLM A/B tests from needing impractically huge sample sizes given how noisy the metrics are?
a: LLM metrics like judge scores are stochastic and heavy-tailed, which inflates variance and therefore the sample size needed to detect a given effect, so the lever is reducing per-unit variance. Use within-subject/paired designs (same inputs through both variants) and variance-reduction like CUPED that regresses out pre-experiment covariates, which cuts required samples without biasing the estimate. Use deterministic decoding where the product allows it to remove sampling noise, and pick a metric that's less skewed (pass-rate over a raw 1-5 mean). You still run a power analysis from baseline, MDE, and the reduced variance to set the actual sample size.
fs: Attacks required-N by reducing variance (paired design, CUPED, deterministic decoding) before powering the test.
fj: Just collects more and more samples without addressing the variance driving the requirement.

q: When validating an LLM judge against human labels, what agreement target is reasonable and what do you do if you fall short?
a: A common practical bar is having domain experts label a representative sample (often around 200 cases) and iterating the judge prompt until it reaches roughly 85-90% agreement with experts, measured with a chance-corrected coefficient like kappa, not raw percent. If you fall short, the usual culprit is an underspecified rubric, so you tighten criteria, add worked positive/negative examples, and decompose the judgment (QAG/decision-tree style) before blaming the model. If agreement still won't move, your humans may not agree either, which caps any achievable judge quality and sends you back to fix the rubric and adjudicate labels. Re-measure after every judge-prompt change.
fs: Cites an ~85-90% expert-agreement target via kappa and treats shortfalls as rubric/decomposition problems.
fj: Accepts the judge with no agreement target, or reads raw percent agreement as good enough.

q: What's the difference between using an LLM judge as an offline scorer versus an online guardrail, and what changes operationally?
a: Offline, the judge scores a fixed golden set in CI where latency and cost barely matter, so you can use a slow, expensive frontier judge with chain-of-thought and pairwise comparisons to gate merges. Online, the same judging logic runs on live traffic in the request path or async monitoring, where its own latency, cost, and failure modes become production concerns, pushing you to a cheaper/faster judge or sampling rather than judging every call. Online judges also need fallback behavior (fail-open vs fail-closed) and can't rely on ground truth, so they lean on reference-free checks like faithfulness or toxicity. You validate both against humans, but the online one trades some accuracy for speed and resilience.
fs: Contrasts a heavy offline CI judge with a cheap, sampled, fail-safe online judge under latency/cost constraints.
fj: Assumes you can run the same expensive offline judge on every production request for free.
